justification: All three input sources (Bird, MJ, Pippen) are clearly represented. All 3 ACs present with exact values (4.50, 5.00, 0.01). Pippen's operational notes explicitly included: JSDoc is a mandatory requirement ('must be able to understand the constraint'), high-traffic context stated (~500 req/sec), unit tests only specified. The $0.01 floor is labeled as a domain invariant in domain_rules and terminology_alignment. Quantity boundary >= 10 preserved exactly in both domain_rules and terminology_alignment. No invented information — the open_questions and floating-point note are legitimate implementation concerns flagged transparently.

observations.text: JSDoc requirement is framed as mandatory ('must be able to understand the constraint without reading domain docs'), not optional
AC3 preserved with exact $0.01 value and explicit 'never zero, never negative' constraint
Terminology alignment section bridges domain and technical language cleanly, including the floor as Math.max(discountedPrice, 0.01)
Proactively flagged floating-point rounding risk that no input agent addressed — adds value without inventing requirements
High-traffic context (~500 req/sec) carried through to task_context, reinforcing stateless/synchronous constraint
Quantity boundary >= 10 appears in domain_rules, terminology_alignment, and AC1/AC2 — no imprecision

handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement a pure TypeScript function `recalculatePrice(quantity, basePrice)`
    that applies bulk discount logic and a price floor. This function will be
    called inline from a high-traffic pricing endpoint (~500 req/sec peak), so
    it must remain stateless, synchronous, and free of any I/O or external
    dependencies.

  domain_rules:
    - rule: Final price = base price minus applicable discounts, floored at $0.01
      testa
